text.title: Burn Review — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn the weekly burn review into an effective architectural ritual.

rule.statement: The review runs WEEKLY at a fixed slot (same day, same time). Cancellation requires a written reason in the decision log; skipping two weeks in a row triggers a stand-down review.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook reliability rituals; behavioural science of cadence

rule.rationale: Irregular reviews allow burn to accumulate unnoticed for weeks. Weekly cadence catches drift within the smallest meaningful window. The cancellation log creates accountability.

rule.statement: Every review walks the same five items: (1) past-week burn trace per SLO, (2) matrix-triggered actions + overrides, (3) postmortem actions due, (4) architectural decisions invited, (5) recontract candidates. Skipping any one is forbidden.
rule.rationale: A fixed agenda removes "what should we discuss?" overhead. Each section guarantees a different lens. Without item 4, the review degenerates into ops status reporting; without item 5, SLOs ossify.

rule.statement: Every review produces ≥1 decision-log entry. Reviews that conclude with "let us think about it" without a recorded decision are forbidden. Deferral IS a decision and is logged as such.

rationale.source: GTD principle of explicit decisions; field experience with status-meeting drift

rule.rationale: A weekly meeting without decisions teaches the team that meetings are theatre. The decision-output rule makes the meeting's value visible; even a deferred decision is documented and trackable.

rule.statement: Required attendees: architect (or tech lead with architectural authority), on-call rotation lead, product representative. The meeting cannot proceed without all three; reschedule rather than substitute.
rule.rationale: SLO decisions are simultaneously technical, operational, and product trade-offs. Missing one role produces lopsided decisions: pure tech (over-engineering), pure ops (cost focus), pure product (feature focus). The triad balances.

rule.statement: The review note is written DURING the meeting and committed to the repo within 2h of meeting end. Verbal-only reviews are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Without the written log, decisions evaporate. A team member out sick that week has no path to catch up. The written log is the audit trail and the catch-up artifact.

example.title: A 30-minute review that spawned an ADR
example.code: Item 1: past-week burn — checkout service burned 18% (slow-burn 72h pattern). Item 2: matrix actions — focus_shift triggered Wed; reverted Fri. Item 3: postmortems — async job retry-storm postmortem still has action #2 open. Item 4: architectural decision — the retry-storm root cause keeps recurring; we have shipped 3 mitigations in 6 months that did not eliminate the pattern. Decision: introduce a global retry budget at the gateway. Spawn ADR-0078 to capture the design. Item 5: recontract — the search service SLO is consistently met but users complain about quality, not latency. Recontract candidate: replace availability SLO with quality SLO. Owner: search-team lead. Decision-log entries: 2 (ADR-0078 spawn, recontract candidate raised).

example.title: The reliability-status standup that decided nothing
example.note: r2 violated (skipped items 3-5), r3 violated (no decision output).
